text: Giovanni Lo Porto, an Italian aid worker, was abducted in Pakistan in 2012 while working for a German aid organization. He was killed in a US drone strike two years later, along with another hostage, American Warren Weinstein. Lo Porto studied peace and conflict studies in London and was passionate about helping others. He was taken captive by al Qaeda, but the group denied any involvement. The Italian government and media pressed for his release, but he was never freed. The US government acknowledged responsibility for his death, which was met with anger from the Italian government. Lo Porto's family and colleagues were devastated by his loss, and his mother was left hoping for her son's safe return.

text: "Grey's Anatomy" character Dr. Derek Shepherd, played by Patrick Dempsey, died following a car crash in last Thursday's episode, leaving his TV wife Dr. Meredith Grey (Ellen Pompeo) as the show's mainstay as it concludes its 11th season next month. Dempsey still has a year left on his contract, and said of the show, "It's been a remarkable ride. The worldwide impact has been extraordinary. It is incredible how devoted and passionate the fans are, and I will forever be grateful and humbled by the experience." Dempsey has a development deal with ABC Studios, "Grey's" producers.

text: Sabeen Mahmud, social advocate and founder of a Karachi-based cafe that served as a hub for social discourse and art, was murdered in a drive-by shooting on Friday. The attack came just hours after she had hosted a forum on human rights abuses in the restive province of Baluchistan, a subject that the Pakistani government discourages public discussion. Mahmud, 40, was shot five times and died on her way to the hospital. Her mother, who was with her in the car, was wounded and is in critical condition. No one has claimed responsibility for the attack. Mahmud's cafe, T2F (The Second Floor), had become a vital space for open discussion and she had become a figurehead for love, tolerance and humanism. Her murder has shocked the community and caused an outpouring of grief across the country.

text: Sabeen Mahmud, a Pakistani activist and owner of a cafe called The Second Floor (T2F), was shot and killed in Karachi by two gunmen. Mahmud was known for her outspokenness on topics such as technology, human rights, and women's entrepreneurship. She was seen as a figurehead for progressive values and free speech in Pakistan. Mahmud had just finished leading a discussion group on a sensitive topic when she was shot. The discussion, which focused on the province of Baluchistan and its alleged human rights abuses, had been shut down by authorities at the Lahore University of Management Sciences. Mahmud defiantly moved the discussion to her cafe, despite receiving death threats in the past for handling the topic. Her killing has sparked grief and gratitude for her work on social media. Mahmud's cafe, T2F, was a gathering place for those seeking secular wisdom and freedom of thought. The walls outside the cafe were decorated with socially critical graffiti. Mahmud's death has raised concerns about the lack of free speech and media censorship in Pakistan.
